items.title: [EPIC] Unify RTPS and shared-memory transport backends into a single individual-to-individual state delivery pipeline
items.body:
## Description

@pub-sub-loop-transport opened · P0 🔴 · EPIC · Claude-D

The transport layer currently exposes two entirely separate code paths for moving individual state between participants in the world model:

1. **RTPS (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe)** — the DDS/RTPS wire protocol used for inter-process and inter-node individual-to-individual communication. Each individual publishes its position, velocity, orientation, and custom attribute data via RTPS `DataWriter`; subscribers on remote nodes receive state updates via `DataReader` with QoS-based filtering (deadline, liveliness, reliability).

2. **Shared-memory segment transport** — the intra-node fast path. When publisher and subscriber individuals reside in the same OS process or on the same NUMA node, state payloads are written into a lock-free shared-memory ring buffer (`transport::ShmSegment<IndividualState>`) and read via `transport::ShmReader`, bypassing serialization entirely.

These two backends share no common abstraction. A `component::IndividualDriver` that needs to publish state must today select a transport at init time via `transport::ParticipantConfig::backend_type`, and the selection is static for the lifetime of that individual's node. This creates three concrete problems:

### Problem 1: No runtime transport migration

When two individuals that were on separate nodes (using RTPS) get co-located onto the same node by the scheduler (e.g. after a world partition rebalance), their pub/sub channel continues to pay the RTPS serialization + UDP loopback cost. There is no mechanism to detect co-location and migrate the channel to shared-memory mid-session.

### Problem 2: Duplicated channel plumbing

`transport::RtpsWriter<T>` and `transport::ShmWriter<T>` share ~60% of their code (QoS enforcement, history cache, deadline tracking, sequence numbering) but are implemented as independent class hierarchies. Bug fixes in one backend routinely fail to be ported to the other.

### Problem 3: Multi-backend fan-out

An individual that needs to publish state to both local subscribers (shared-memory) and remote subscribers (RTPS) must today instantiate two writers and manually dispatch. The `data::ChannelWriter<T>` abstraction does not support multi-backend fan-out.

## Proposed Design

Introduce `transport::UnifiedWriter<T>` and `transport::UnifiedReader<T>` that sit above both backends:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           transport::UnifiedWriter<T>           │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌───────────────┐ │
│  │ QoS      │  │ History  │  │ Sequence      │ │
│  │ Enforcer │  │ Cache    │  │ Numbering     │ │
│  └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘  └──────┬────────┘ │
│       └──────────────┼───────────────┘          │
│              ┌───────▼────────┐                 │
│              │ BackendRouter  │                 │
│              └───┬────────┬───┘                 │
│          ┌───────▼──┐ ┌───▼──────────┐          │
│          │ ShmSink  │ │  RtpsSink    │          │
│          └──────────┘ └──────────────┘          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### BackendRouter dispatch policy

`BackendRouter` maintains a routing table keyed by subscriber individual ID:

```cpp
struct RouteEntry {
    IndividualId subscriber;
    TransportBackend backend;     // Shm | Rtps | IntraProcess
    uint64_t topology_distance;   // hop count in world graph
    size_t last_payload_bytes;
    Timestamp last_migration_check;
};
```

On every `write(state)` call:
1. For each active subscriber, `BackendRouter::select_backend()` evaluates:
   - Is subscriber in-process? → `IntraProcess` (zero-copy pointer handoff)
   - Is subscriber on same NUMA node? → `Shm` (shared-memory segment)
   - Otherwise → `Rtps` (serialized wire protocol)
2. State payload is dispatched to the appropriate sink. If multiple backends are active (fan-out), serialization happens at most once (lazy).

### Runtime migration protocol

When `service_discovery` detects a topology change (individual migrated to a new node), it publishes a `TopologyDelta` message on the `__world/topology` channel. `BackendRouter` subscribes to this channel and:

1. Marks affected routes as `MIGRATING`
2. Drains in-flight messages on the old backend (waits for ACKs or deadline)
3. Establishes the new backend connection
4. Atomically swaps the route entry
5. Publishes a `RouteMigrated` event for profiler instrumentation

The migration is transparent to the individual's component code — `UnifiedWriter::write()` blocks briefly during the swap (bounded by `transport::migration_timeout`, default 50ms).

### Shared code consolidation

Factor the common logic into `transport::WriterCore<T>`:
- `QosEnforcer` — deadline/liveliness/reliability policy (currently duplicated)
- `HistoryCache<T>` — bounded ring of past states for late-joining subscribers
- `SequenceCounter` — monotonic sequence numbering with overflow wrapping
- `transport::Metrics` — bytes written, messages dropped, latency histogram

`ShmSink` and `RtpsSink` become thin adapters (~200 LOC each) that only handle the actual data movement.

## Dependencies

- `service_discovery` module must publish `TopologyDelta` events (see issue: Add topology change notification to ServiceDiscovery)
- `data::ChannelWriter<T>` needs to be refactored to delegate to `UnifiedWriter<T>` instead of directly holding a backend
- `profiler` module needs new trace points for migration events

## Scope and sub-tasks

1. Define `transport::UnifiedWriter<T>` / `UnifiedReader<T>` interface and `BackendRouter` routing table — **P0**
2. Extract `WriterCore<T>` from existing `RtpsWriter` and `ShmWriter` — **P0**
3. Implement `IntraProcess` backend (zero-copy pointer handoff for same-process individuals) — **P1**
4. Implement runtime migration protocol with `TopologyDelta` subscription — **P1**
5. Migrate `data::ChannelWriter<T>` to use `UnifiedWriter<T>` — **P0**
6. Add profiler trace points for backend selection and migration events — **P2**
7. Benchmarks: measure latency overhead of `BackendRouter` dispatch vs direct backend — **P2**
8. Documentation: transport backend selection algorithm, migration protocol sequence diagram — **P2**

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A single `UnifiedWriter<T>` can deliver state to subscribers on mixed backends (some Shm, some RTPS) without the individual's component code being aware
- [ ] When two individuals are moved to the same node, their channel migrates from RTPS to Shm within 100ms with zero message loss
- [ ] QoS enforcement (deadline, reliability) works identically across all backends
- [ ] Latency overhead of `BackendRouter` dispatch is <500ns per write compared to direct backend call
- [ ] All existing transport unit tests pass against `UnifiedWriter` with no behavioral change

## Activity

- Claude-D assigned to lead implementation
- Sprint: Current
- Blocked by: service_discovery topology notification API

items.body:
## Description
Data 模块中 `world::std::all_of` 用于批量校验个体状态一致性时，性能显著劣于朴素循环归约。分析发现 all_of 内部调用路径未触发 ISG（warp）向量化，每次谓词调用产生单独的函数调用开销，导致大规模个体状态校验时吞吐量下降 3-5x。

```cpp
// 当前慢路径（未向量化）
bool all_valid = world::std::all_of(
    individual_states.begin(), individual_states.end(),
    [](const IndividualState& s) { return s.is_valid(); }
);
// ISG warp 内未向量化，逐个调用 is_valid()

// 优化目标：ISG 并行向量化
// warp 内 32 个 lane 同时检测，单次 __ballot_sync 归约
```

## Dependencies
- ISG（warp）ballot 归约原语
- world::std::all_of 实现
- 个体状态谓词向量化框架

## Sub-tasks
- [ ] 分析 all_of 未向量化的根因（函数调用 vs lambda 内联）
- [ ] 强制 lambda 谓词内联（`__forceinline__` + `__device__`）
- [ ] 实现 ISG warp-ballot 加速路径：`__ballot_sync` 批量检测
- [ ] 基准测试：1M 个体状态 all_of，对比优化前后吞吐量
- [ ] 回归测试：谓词语义正确性验证

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] all_of 吞吐量 >= 朴素循环归约的 0.9x（不再劣于朴素路径）
- [ ] ISG warp-ballot 路径在 sm_80+ 设备上自动激活
- [ ] 谓词语义不变：全部 true 返回 true，有 false 即返回 false
- [ ] 短路语义保留：检测到 false 后尽快退出
- [ ] 基准测试报告纳入 CI 性能回归检测

items.title: [BUG] Scheduler随机Tick分配器uint64粒度下仅产生4096倍数间隔——大规模个体更新聚集于固定时间片边界导致突发峰值
items.body:
## Description
Scheduler 随机 Tick 分配器（RandomTickAllocator）在 uint64 粒度下，由于内部对齐掩码 `& ~0xFFF`（4096 对齐），实际只产生 4096 的倍数间隔。大规模个体的 tick 时间聚集在固定时间片边界，形成周期性突发峰值（burst），导致世界状态处理器（GPU）负载尖刺。

```cpp
// BUG：强制 4096 对齐导致 tick 聚集
uint64_t alloc_tick(uint64_t base, uint64_t range) {
    uint64_t raw = base + (rand_u64() % range);
    return raw & ~0xFFFULL;  // BUG: 4096 对齐，所有个体聚集到同一边界
}

// 修复：去除对齐掩码，使用真随机偏移
uint64_t alloc_tick(uint64_t base, uint64_t range) {
    return base + (rand_u64() % range);  // 均匀分布
}
```

## Dependencies
- Scheduler RandomTickAllocator 实现
- 世界 tick 负载分析工具（profiler）
- ISG（warp）tick 分配策略

## Sub-tasks
- [ ] 定位并移除 RandomTickAllocator 中的 4096 对齐掩码
- [ ] 验证修复后 tick 分布均匀性（卡方检验 p > 0.05）
- [ ] 在 profiler 中新增 tick 分布直方图可视化
- [ ] 压测：100K 个体调度，验证负载均匀性
- [ ] 检查其他 Scheduler 路径是否存在类似对齐问题

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 修复后 tick 间隔不再聚集于 4096 倍数边界
- [ ] tick 分布卡方检验均匀性 p > 0.05
- [ ] 世界状态处理器 tick 负载峰值降低 > 50%
- [ ] 100K 个体调度延迟 p99 降低（无突发峰值）
- [ ] 其他 Scheduler 路径无类似对齐问题

items.body:
## Description
`scheduler::croutine::block_scan` 在 alg_1/mode_0 配置下，对 `uint8x3`（3 字节非对齐）消息类型输出错误结果。根因是 block_scan 内部假设消息类型为 2 的幂次字节对齐，3 字节类型在 ISG（warp）归约边界处发生位域错位，导致扫描累积值损坏。

```cpp
// 复现示例
uint8x3 input[64] = { {1,2,3}, {4,5,6}, ... };
// alg_1/mode_0 block_scan 后：
// 期望：前缀和累积正确
// 实际：3字节边界处高字节被截断或与相邻元素混叠

// 根因：内部以 uint32_t 载入 uint8x3，3字节类型需特殊处理
uint32_t loaded = *reinterpret_cast<uint32_t*>(&input[i]);
// BUG: 读取 4 字节但类型只有 3 字节，越界且混叠
```

## Dependencies
- block_scan alg_1/mode_0 实现
- uint8x3 类型定义与对齐规范
- ISG（warp）归约原语

## Sub-tasks
- [ ] 添加 uint8x3 对齐问题的最小复现用例
- [ ] 修复：为非 2^n 字节类型添加安全的逐字节载入路径
- [ ] 泛化修复：处理所有非 2^n 对齐的消息类型
- [ ] 在 block_scan 中添加 static_assert 或运行时 assert 检测对齐问题
- [ ] 回归测试：uint8x3 以及其他非对齐类型（uint8x5、uint8x3_pair）

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] uint8x3 在 alg_1/mode_0 下 block_scan 输出正确前缀和
- [ ] 非 2^n 字节类型均走安全载入路径
- [ ] 修复后无越界内存访问（ASAN 验证）
- [ ] 对齐类型（uint8/uint16/uint32）性能不受影响
- [ ] 所有非对齐类型通过完整 block_scan 测试套件

items.title: [FEA] data::transform_reduce 二元-二元重载 — 新增双源 pub/sub 通道并行聚合的 binary-binary 变换归约接口
items.body:
## Description
在 `world::data` 模块实现 `transform_reduce` 的 binary-binary 重载，支持从两个 pub/sub 源通道（source channels）并行读取个体状态，逐元素应用二元变换（binary transform），再进行二元归约（binary reduce），适用于多源传感器数据融合等场景。

```cpp
namespace world::data {
  // binary-binary transform_reduce
  template <typename InputIt1, typename InputIt2,
            typename T, typename BinaryOp, typename BinaryTransform>
  T transform_reduce(
      InputIt1 first1, InputIt1 last1,  // 源通道 1（如 lidar 状态）
      InputIt2 first2,                   // 源通道 2（如 camera 状态）
      T init,                            // 归约初始值
      BinaryOp reduce_op,                // 归约算子（如 world::plus）
      BinaryTransform transform_op       // 变换算子（如 fusion_weight）
  );
}

// 用法：多传感器融合加权和
auto fused = world::data::transform_reduce(
    lidar_states.begin(), lidar_states.end(),
    camera_states.begin(),
    IndividualState{},          // 初始值
    world::state_merge{},       // 归约：状态合并
    [](const auto& l, const auto& c) { return weighted_fuse(l, c, 0.6f, 0.4f); }
);
```

## Dependencies
- world::std::transform_reduce 标准实现
- ISG（warp）双源并行读取原语
- pub/sub 双通道迭代器

## Sub-tasks
- [ ] 实现 binary-binary transform_reduce 模板函数
- [ ] ISG 并行化：两源同步读取 + warp 归约
- [ ] 验证与串行 transform_reduce 结果等价
- [ ] 性能基准：双源融合 vs 两次单源 transform_reduce
- [ ] 文档：binary-binary 重载与单源版本的区别

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] binary-binary transform_reduce 接口与 world::std 规范一致
- [ ] ISG 并行路径结果与串行版本完全一致
- [ ] 双源融合性能 >= 两次单源版本的 1.4x
- [ ] 两源长度不一致时编译期或运行时安全报错
- [ ] 文档示例覆盖传感器融合典型场景

items.body:
## Description
`IndividualPipeline::DeviceScan`（个体间流水线传播扫描）在处理非 tile 对齐的个体集合时，对越界（out-of-bounds）的填充元素调用归约算子，导致非有效个体数据参与扫描计算，产生错误的前缀和结果。

```cpp
// 问题：tile 填充元素参与归约
// 设 num_individuals = 33，tile_size = 32
// tile 1: [0..31] 有效
// tile 2: [32]    有效，[33..63] 为填充——BUG: 填充元素被 scan_op 调用

// 修复：尾部 tile 掩码保护
for (int lane = 0; lane < TILE_SIZE; ++lane) {
    bool valid = (tile_offset + lane) < num_individuals;
    auto val = valid ? input[tile_offset + lane] : identity;  // 无效元素用恒元
    acc = scan_op(acc, val);   // 仅有效元素参与（或用恒元）
}
```

## Dependencies
- IndividualPipeline::DeviceScan 实现
- ISG（warp）掩码保护原语
- world::std scan 语义规范

## Sub-tasks
- [ ] 在尾部 tile 处理中添加有效性掩码保护
- [ ] 无效元素使用扫描算子的恒元（identity element）
- [ ] 新增 num_individuals 为非 tile_size 倍数的边界测试
- [ ] 验证修复后尾部 tile 扫描结果与参考实现一致
- [ ] 检查同族算子（DeviceReduce）是否存在相同问题

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 非 tile 对齐个体集合扫描结果与参考实现完全一致
- [ ] 越界填充元素不再调用归约算子（日志验证）
- [ ] 边界测试：num_individuals = 1/31/32/33/63/64/65
- [ ] DeviceReduce 同步检查并修复（如有问题）
- [ ] ASAN 无越界内存访问

items.title: [FEA] Document individual parallel execution policies: par_nosync (fire-and-forget) vs par (synchronized) tick modes in scheduler
items.body:
# [FEA] Document Individual Parallel Execution Policies: par_nosync vs par Tick Modes

## Summary
Write comprehensive documentation for the two parallel execution policies available to individuals in the scheduler module: `par_nosync` (fire-and-forget asynchronous tick) and `par` (barrier-synchronized tick). These policies determine how an individual's update loop interleaves with the world tick.

## Motivation
Developers creating new individual types need clear guidance on when to use synchronized vs asynchronous tick execution. Incorrect policy choice leads to either unnecessary blocking (hurting real-time performance) or state inconsistency (individuals reading stale peer state). This is especially critical for embedded targets (RK3568) where CPU budget is tight.

## Requirements

### Documentation Scope
- **`par` (synchronized) policy**: Individual's tick completes before the world advances. Guarantees that when individual A reads individual B's state, it sees B's state from the current or immediately previous tick. Use for tightly-coupled individuals (e.g., parent reading child sub-individual state).
- **`par_nosync` (fire-and-forget) policy**: Individual's tick is dispatched asynchronously. The world tick does not wait for completion. Use for loosely-coupled individuals where eventual consistency is acceptable (e.g., logging individuals, statistics aggregators).
- Comparison table: latency impact, state consistency guarantees, CPU core affinity behavior
- Code examples showing both policies in individual component declarations
- Migration guide: how to switch an individual from `par` to `par_nosync` and vice versa
- Performance characteristics on embedded (RK3568) vs desktop targets

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Published in project documentation site (Markdown + diagrams)
- [ ] Includes at least 3 code examples with annotated comments
- [ ] Reviewed by scheduler module owner
- [ ] Addresses the state consistency implications clearly

## Design Notes
- Include sequence diagrams showing tick ordering under both policies
- Reference the `croutine` module for coroutine-based tick implementation
- Cross-reference `blocker` module for intra-process synchronization between sub-individuals using `par` mode

## Dependencies
- `scheduler` module implementation
- `croutine` module (coroutine tick loops)
- `blocker` module (sync primitives)

## Cyber RT Module Mapping
**scheduler** — Tick loop scheduling, which individuals update when

## Description

### 世界模型定位
`world::data` 模块史诗级需求：Design top-k priority selection framework for individual state buffers - rank and filter world entities by relevance score for focused pub/sub delivery

### pub/sub-loop 上下文
世界模型数据层——个体状态通道缓冲区、变换归约、多源融合，是 pub/sub 数据流的核心处理管道。本需求服务于世界模型的史诗级需求目标，确保个体（individual）在世界中持续更新数据和位置时，相关功能正确、高效、可靠。

### 个体输出动态关联
data 模块将多个个体的原始状态数据融合为一致的世界视图，这个视图最终驱动 needle-tools 的 GLB 场景渲染。

items.title: [FEA] Implement stream-ordered mixed pub/sub channel and intra-process tick execution example with croutine host_launch pattern
items.body:

items.title: [FEA] Add example mixing legacy Cyber RT transport protocol and modern pub/sub-loop intra-process channel execution in the same world context
items.body:
## Description

@pub-sub-loop-transport · P2 🟢 · FEA · Claude-G

New users of the pub/sub-loop framework struggle to understand how the legacy Cyber RT transport protocol coexists with the modern pub/sub-loop intra-process channel system within the same world context. There is no example showing both protocols operating simultaneously on the same set of individuals.

### The two protocol stacks

**Legacy Cyber RT transport** (still supported for backward compatibility):
- Uses `cyber::Writer<T>` / `cyber::Reader<T>` APIs
- Channel naming: string-based paths (`/cyber/node_name/channel_name`)
- Serialization: protobuf only
- Discovery: `cyber::ServiceDiscovery` with multicast UDP announcement
- Individual states are published as raw protobuf messages with no world-model awareness

**Modern pub/sub-loop** (the target architecture):
- Uses `transport::UnifiedWriter<T>` / `transport::UnifiedReader<T>`
- Channel naming: typed `ChannelId` with individual identity baked in
- Serialization: `message::Serializer<T>` (supports protobuf, flatbuffers, zero-copy)
- Discovery: `service_discovery::TopologyOracle` with individual lifecycle events
- Individual states carry world-tick metadata, topology position, and inter-individual references

### What the example should demonstrate

A "migration bridge" scenario where a world contains both legacy individuals (using Cyber RT APIs) and modern individuals (using pub/sub-loop APIs), and they need to exchange state:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    World Context                      │
│                                                       │
│  ┌─────────────┐          ┌──────────────────┐       │
│  │ Legacy       │  bridge  │ Modern            │      │
│  │ Individual A │ ◄──────► │ Individual B      │      │
│  │              │          │                   │      │
│  │ cyber::Writer│          │ transport::Unified│      │
│  │ protobuf msg │          │ typed state       │      │
│  └─────────────┘          └──────────────────┘       │
│         │                          │                  │
│         ▼                          ▼                  │
│  ┌─────────────┐          ┌──────────────────┐       │
│  │ Legacy       │          │ Modern            │      │
│  │ Individual C │          │ Individual D      │      │
│  │ cyber::Reader│          │ transport::Unified│      │
│  └─────────────┘          └──────────────────┘       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Example structure

```
examples/
  mixed_transport/
    README.md                     # Explanation of the migration scenario
    world_config.yaml             # World context with both protocol stacks
    legacy_individual.cc          # Individual using cyber::Writer/Reader
    modern_individual.cc          # Individual using transport::UnifiedWriter
    bridge_component.cc           # Protocol bridge: translates between APIs
    proto/
      legacy_position.proto       # Legacy protobuf state definition
    state/
      modern_position.h           # Modern typed state definition
    CMakeLists.txt
```

### Bridge component design

```cpp
// bridge_component.cc
//
// Subscribes to legacy Cyber RT channels and republishes
// on modern pub/sub-loop channels (and vice versa)

class ProtocolBridge : public component::IndividualComponent {
public:
    bool Init() override {
        // Legacy side: cyber::Reader for protobuf messages
        legacy_reader_ = cyber_node_->CreateReader<LegacyPosition>(
            "/cyber/legacy_individual/position",
            [this](const auto& msg) { OnLegacyMessage(msg); });
        
        // Modern side: pub/sub-loop writer
        modern_writer_ = CreateWriter<ModernPositionState>(
            ChannelId::For(this->individual_id(), "position"));
        
        return true;
    }

private:
    void OnLegacyMessage(const std::shared_ptr<LegacyPosition>& msg) {
        // Translate legacy protobuf → modern typed state
        ModernPositionState state;
        state.position = {msg->x(), msg->y(), msg->z()};
        state.velocity = {msg->vx(), msg->vy(), msg->vz()};
        state.world_tick = current_tick();  // add world-model metadata
        state.source_individual = resolve_legacy_id(msg->node_name());
        
        modern_writer_->Write(state);
    }
};
```

## Sub-tasks

1. Create `examples/mixed_transport/` directory structure — **P2**
2. Implement `legacy_individual.cc` — publishes position at 10Hz via `cyber::Writer` — **P2**
3. Implement `modern_individual.cc` — publishes position at 30Hz via `transport::UnifiedWriter` — **P2**
4. Implement `bridge_component.cc` — bidirectional translation — **P2**
5. Write `README.md` documenting the migration path from legacy to modern — **P2**
6. Add CMake target and CI build verification — **P3**

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Example compiles and runs as a standalone world with 4 individuals (2 legacy, 2 modern)
- [ ] Bridge component translates legacy→modern and modern→legacy with <1ms added latency
- [ ] README clearly documents which API to use for new individuals and how to migrate existing ones
- [ ] Example runs for 60 seconds without memory leaks or channel deadlocks
- [ ] Legacy individuals can be gradually migrated to modern API by changing one component at a time

items.title: [FEA] Support multi-segment hierarchical individual scanning in scheduler for batch sub-individual state aggregation
items.body:
# [FEA] Support Multi-Segment Hierarchical Individual Scanning in Scheduler

## Summary
Extend the `scheduler` module's segment scan algorithm to process multiple hierarchical segments per scheduling block. This enables efficient batch aggregation of sub-individual states within a parent individual, reducing scheduling overhead when an individual decomposes into many sub-individuals (e.g., a transformer individual with 96 decoder sub-individuals).

## Motivation
Currently, the scheduler processes one segment (one sub-individual group) per scheduling block. When an individual decomposes into N sub-individuals, this results in N scheduling passes to aggregate their states into the parent. For large decompositions (e.g., a neural network individual with hundreds of layer sub-individuals), this creates excessive scheduling overhead and increases tick latency.

By processing multiple segments per block, we can batch sub-individual state aggregation within a single scheduling pass, significantly reducing overhead.

## Requirements

### Functional Requirements
- Extend `AgentSegmentScan` to accept a `segments_per_block` parameter (default: 1 for backward compatibility)
- Implement multi-segment state aggregation: within one scheduling block, scan and merge states from K sub-individuals
- Support variable segment sizes (sub-individuals may have different state sizes)
- Maintain correct ordering guarantees: parent individual sees aggregated sub-individual states in deterministic order
- Support both exclusive (prefix-sum style, each sub-individual sees only predecessors' state) and inclusive (each sees all peers' state) scan modes

### Non-Functional Requirements
- Multi-segment mode should achieve ≥ 2x throughput improvement over single-segment for K ≥ 4
- Zero additional memory allocation in the hot path (pre-allocate segment buffers)
- Must work correctly with both `par` and `par_nosync` execution policies
- Embedded target (RK3568): must not exceed 64KB stack per scheduling coroutine

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `AgentSegmentScan` processes K segments per block correctly
- [ ] Exclusive and inclusive scan modes produce identical results to single-segment reference implementation
- [ ] Benchmark shows ≥ 2x speedup for 16-segment hierarchical individual
- [ ] Stack usage verified under 64KB on aarch64
- [ ] Integration test: transformer individual with 12 decoder sub-individuals uses multi-segment scan

## Design Notes
- The segment scan is analogous to a parallel prefix sum over sub-individual states
- Each "segment" corresponds to a sub-individual's state buffer in the `data` module
- Use the `blocker` module for intra-process synchronization between segments within a block
- Consider warp-level primitives analogy: treat sub-individual groups as "warps" for batched scheduling

## Dependencies
- `scheduler` module (segment scan implementation)
- `data` module (sub-individual state buffers)
- `blocker` module (intra-process sync)
- `component` module (hierarchical individual decomposition)

## Cyber RT Module Mapping
**scheduler** — Tick loop scheduling, which individuals update when

items.title: [FEA] Smoke test for exclusive state prefix-scan across individual hierarchy in data module
items.body:
# [FEA] Smoke Test for Exclusive State Prefix-Scan Across Individual Hierarchy

## Summary
Implement a smoke test verifying the exclusive prefix-scan operation over hierarchical individual states in the `data` module. The exclusive scan computes running aggregates across sub-individuals where each sub-individual sees only the accumulated state of its predecessors, not its own contribution.

## Motivation
The exclusive scan is a fundamental operation for hierarchical individual state aggregation. It is used when sub-individuals need context from predecessors but must not see their own state in the accumulator (e.g., sequential pipeline stages where each stage processes based on all prior stages' outputs). A smoke test ensures basic correctness before deeper integration testing.

## Requirements

### Functional Requirements
- Test case: create a parent individual with 4 sub-individuals, each publishing an integer state
- Verify exclusive scan produces correct prefix sums: [0, a, a+b, a+b+c] for inputs [a, b, c, d]
- Test with identity element (0 for sum, empty for state merge)
- Test edge cases: single sub-individual, empty hierarchy

### Non-Functional Requirements
- Smoke test runs in < 1 second
- No external dependencies beyond the test framework
- Runs on both x86_64 and aarch64

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Basic exclusive scan test passes for sum operator
- [ ] Custom operator (state merge) exclusive scan test passes
- [ ] Edge cases covered (1 element, 0 elements)
- [ ] Test integrated into CTest suite

## Design Notes
- Use `data` module's `DataFusion` facility for multi-source state merging
- The exclusive scan is the complement of the inclusive scan (item [116])
- Both scan types share the same underlying `ChannelBuffer` infrastructure

## Dependencies
- `data` module (state buffering, fusion)
- `component` module (sub-individual hierarchy)

## Cyber RT Module Mapping
**data** — Individual state buffering, fusion of multiple state sources

items.title: [FEA] Smoke test for inclusive state prefix-scan across individual hierarchy in data module
items.body:
# [FEA] Smoke Test for Inclusive State Prefix-Scan Across Individual Hierarchy

## Summary
Implement a smoke test verifying the inclusive prefix-scan operation over hierarchical individual states in the `data` module. The inclusive scan computes running aggregates where each sub-individual sees the accumulated state including its own contribution.

## Motivation
The inclusive scan is the counterpart to the exclusive scan (item [115]). It is used when sub-individuals need full context including their own state in the accumulator (e.g., cumulative position updates where each sub-individual's position depends on all positions including its own). A smoke test ensures basic correctness.

## Requirements

### Functional Requirements
- Test case: create a parent individual with 4 sub-individuals, each publishing an integer state
- Verify inclusive scan produces correct prefix sums: [a, a+b, a+b+c, a+b+c+d] for inputs [a, b, c, d]
- Test with custom merge operator (e.g., position accumulation: each sub-individual adds a delta)
- Test edge cases: single sub-individual, empty hierarchy

### Non-Functional Requirements
- Smoke test runs in < 1 second
- No external dependencies beyond the test framework
- Runs on both x86_64 and aarch64

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Basic inclusive scan test passes for sum operator
- [ ] Custom operator (position delta merge) test passes
- [ ] Edge cases covered (1 element, 0 elements)
- [ ] Test integrated into CTest suite

## Design Notes
- Shares infrastructure with exclusive scan test (item [115])

items.title: [DOC] Fix broken dynamic shared memory reference link in Cyber RT transport documentation
items.body:
## Description

@pub-sub-loop-transport · P3 · DOC · Claude-E

### Problem

The Cyber RT transport documentation for dynamic shared memory usage contains a broken reference link. The documentation page `transport/shared_memory.md` references the dynamic shared memory allocation API with a link:

```markdown
For details on dynamic shared memory allocation, see 
[Dynamic Shared Memory API](/docs/transport/dynamic_shm_api.md).
```

The target file `/docs/transport/dynamic_shm_api.md` does not exist. It was renamed to `/docs/transport/runtime_shm_allocation.md` during the shared-memory refactoring (commit `a3f2e1d`) but the referencing page was not updated.

### Fix

Update the link in `transport/shared_memory.md`:

```markdown
<!-- Before -->
[Dynamic Shared Memory API](/docs/transport/dynamic_shm_api.md)

<!-- After -->
[Runtime Shared Memory Allocation](/docs/transport/runtime_shm_allocation.md)
```

Additionally, audit all documentation under `docs/transport/` for other broken internal links:

### Audit scope

```
docs/transport/
├── overview.md                    # links to check: 12
├── shared_memory.md               # known broken link (this issue)
├── rtps_protocol.md               # links to check: 8
├── channel_configuration.md       # links to check: 5
├── runtime_shm_allocation.md      # (target file, check backlinks)
├── message_format.md              # links to check: 6
└── async_operations.md            # links to check: 4
```

## Dependencies

- No code dependencies — documentation fix only
- Relates to: tools CI (should add a link-checker to prevent future broken links)

## Sub-tasks

1. Fix the known broken link in `shared_memory.md` — **P1**
2. Run link checker across all `docs/transport/` files — **P2**
3. Fix any additional broken links found — **P2**
4. Add `tools/check_doc_links.py` script to CI — **P3**

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] The link from `shared_memory.md` to the dynamic shared memory API resolves correctly
- [ ] Zero broken internal links in `docs/transport/` directory
- [ ] CI link-checker catches future broken links before merge
